The camp was to be cleansed. The purity of the church must be kept as carefully as its peace and order. Every polluted Israelite must be separated. Wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceful. The more religious profession a house or family makes, the more they are obligated to put away wickedness far from them. If a man wrongs or defrauds his brother in any matter, it is a trespass against the Lord, who strictly charges and commands us to do justly. What is to be done when a man's awakened conscience accuses him of this kind of guilt, even if it happened long ago? He must confess his sin, confess it to God, confess it to his neighbor, and take shame to himself; though it goes against him to admit he was lying, yet he must do it. Satisfaction must be made for the offense done to God, as well as for the loss sustained by the neighbor; restitution in that case is not enough without faith and repentance. While something wrongly obtained is knowingly kept, the guilt remains on the conscience, and is not removed by sacrifice or offering, prayers or tears; for it is the same act of sin persisted in. This is the doctrine of right reason, and of the word of God. It exposes hypocrites, and directs the tender conscience to proper conduct, which, springing from faith in Christ, will make way for inward peace.

This law would make the women of Israel watch against giving cause for suspicion. On the other hand, it would hinder the cruel treatment such suspicions might occasion. It would also hinder the guilty from escaping, and the innocent from coming under just suspicion. When no proof could be brought, the wife was called on to make this solemn appeal to a heart-searching God. No woman, if she were guilty, could say “Amen” to the adjuration, and drink the water after it, unless she disbelieved the truth of God, or defied his justice. The water is called the bitter water, because it caused the curse. Thus sin is called an evil and a bitter thing. Let all who meddle with forbidden pleasures, know that they will be bitterness in the end. From the whole learn: 1. Secret sins are known to God, and sometimes are strangely brought to light in this life; and that there is a day coming when God will, by Christ, judge the secrets of men according to the gospel. 2 In particular, those who commit adultery and are unfaithful God will surely judge. Though we do not now have the waters of jealousy, yet we have God's word, which ought to be as great a terror. Sensual lusts will end in bitterness. 3. God will manifest the innocence of the innocent. The same providence is for good to some, and for hurt to others. And it will answer the purposes which God intends.
